meta.title: Meriq — AI operations layer for customs brokers & freight forwarders
meta.description: Meriq turns shipment emails, invoices, packing lists, and bills of lading into source-backed, ready-to-file case reviews — surfacing missing documents, HS / CCC classification questions, permit risks, and customer-ready status updates for customs brokers, freight forwarders, and trade teams.

hero.eyebrow: For customs brokers, forwarders & in-house trade teams
hero.titleLead: Your shipments don't get ready by themselves.
hero.pitch: Meriq works your pre-filing queue: supplier emails, missing documents, HS / CCC questions, permit risks, and follow-ups.
hero.outcome: Your team reviews the case, approves the follow-up, and moves the shipment forward.

trustBar.lead: Built for customs brokers, freight forwarders, and trade operations teams handling document-heavy shipments.

hiddenCost.title: Pre-filing work is where shipments get stuck
hiddenCost.subtitle: Before a single entry is filed, your team is already buried in back-and-forth. That's where the time leaks and delays begin.

items.title: Scattered across inboxes
items.desc: Supplier emails, attachments, and replies spread across threads no one fully owns.

items.title: Missing documents found too late
items.desc: A missing COO or permit surfaces the day before filing — not the day it arrived.

items.title: Invoice / packing-list mismatches
items.desc: Quantities, weights, and values quietly disagree across documents.

items.title: Agency / inspection readiness uncertainty
items.desc: Whether an agency permit, inspection, or prior notice is needed often isn't clear until it's urgent.

items.title: The same information, chased again
items.desc: Operators re-ask suppliers for details already buried in an earlier email.

items.title: Value & disbursement basis is hard to trace
items.desc: Declared values, estimated duties, and possible disbursements are scattered across invoices, the entry, payment slips, terminal charges, and email. When a customer asks about an amount, the team digs back through documents for the basis.

howItWorks.title: Send Meriq a shipment. It does the review. You approve the next step.
howItWorks.subtitle: Three steps from messy intake to a broker-ready case.

steps.title: Upload or share a shipment case
steps.desc: Upload recent supplier emails, invoices, packing lists, B/Ls, product details, and related compliance documents. Meriq organizes the case, flags gaps and questions, and produces a reviewable result.

steps.title: Meriq reviews the case
steps.desc: Meriq organizes the documents, detects missing information, checks invoice and packing-list mismatches, flags pre-filing blockers, and drafts supplier follow-ups.

steps.title: Your team approves the next step
steps.desc: Review the outcome, approve follow-ups, and move the case toward ready-to-file with every issue traceable back to source evidence.

watch.title: Before your team calls the authority, Meriq prepares the case.
watch.subtitle: Meriq surfaces likely HS / CCC candidates, missing product details, and source-backed questions for broker review — before your team files.

blockers.title: RF / wireless device?
blockers.desc: Catalog mentions Wi-Fi / Bluetooth; docs don't confirm

blockers.title: HS / CCC under RF telecom control?
Product specs incomplete
blockers.desc: Missing datasheet, frequency, output power, full model, brand, NCC cert

chips: Can this case be filed now?
If we file now, will we get a correction request?
What's missing to confirm the HS / CCC code?
Where do the declared value and estimated duties come from?
Draft the next questions to ask.
watch.note: Meriq surfaces likely requirements and missing evidence. Your team makes the final filing decision.

chat.text: I've read the commercial invoice, packing list, B/L, product photos, and catalog, and compared the main fields.

I found 2 things to confirm first: the invoice says “Wireless IoT Gateway” while the packing list says “RF Communication Module” — the product names don't fully match. And the catalog mentions Wi-Fi / Bluetooth, but the documents don't include the frequency, output power, or full model spec.

I'd add the missing details first. Two things are blocking it:
First, the invoice and packing-list product names disagree, which affects classification.
Second, the product may include RF functions, but the documents aren't enough to confirm the HS / CCC classification or whether an agency or customs-assisted inspection applies.
If we file now, are we likely to get a correction request?

Possibly. The most likely requests are product specs and agency-related documents.
The documents only show it's a Wireless IoT Gateway — they can't confirm whether it's an RF device, a comms module, or general networking gear. Different classifications can mean different import rules.
I'd confirm the specs with the supplier first, then let your broker decide whether to ask the authority.
Draft the next questions to ask.

Done. I prepared two sets of reviewable questions — one for the supplier to fill gaps, one to confirm direction with the authority.

Supplier questions (draft):
Please confirm whether this Wireless IoT Gateway includes Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, RF, LTE, or other wireless functions. Please provide the full model number, product datasheet, frequency range, output power, intended use, manufacturer spec sheet, and any existing NCC or type-approval documents.

Authority question (draft):
The documents list the product as a Wireless IoT Gateway, and the catalog mentions Wi-Fi / Bluetooth, but frequency, output power, and full model data are still missing. Could you confirm whether, under this product description, RF / telecom-controlled equipment rules may apply, or whether further agency documents are needed for pre-filing review.
What should we tell the customer?

Your shipment is still under pre-filing review.
Two items need confirming first: the invoice and packing-list product names don't match, and the product may include wireless RF functions but the documents don't yet provide full frequency, output power, model, and certification data.
We've compiled the product specs to request from the supplier and prepared the authority-confirmation questions. Once the details come back, it can move to broker review.

pilotCta.title: Test Meriq with 5 real shipment cases.
pilotCta.subtitle: We turn each shipment into a pre-filing case review: missing documents, field mismatches, HS / CCC questions, likely permit or authority issues, and follow-up drafts you can send to customers or suppliers.
pilotCta.note: Pilot · no tool switching · validated on real cases

items.a: Meriq is an AI operations layer for customs brokers and freight forwarders. It reviews shipment emails, invoices, packing lists, and bills of lading before filing — surfacing missing documents, HS / CCC classification questions, permit risks, and customer-ready status updates.
Customs brokers, freight forwarders, and in-house import/export and trade operations teams that handle document-heavy shipments.

items.q: Does Meriq file customs entries for me?
items.a: No. Meriq handles the pre-filing work — organizing documents, cross-checking fields, surfacing likely HS / CCC and permit questions, and drafting supplier and authority follow-ups. Your team makes the final classification and filing decisions.

items.q: How does Meriq handle HS / CCC classification?
items.a: Meriq surfaces likely HS / CCC candidates from the product details it can read, flags what's missing to confirm the code, and drafts the questions to ask suppliers or the relevant authority. It does not make the final legal determination.

items.q: Is my shipment data kept traceable?
items.a: Yes. Every extracted field and finding links back to its source document, so your team can verify everything. During the pilot we review your real cases under a direct agreement with your team.
Apply for a pilot. Send us 5 real shipment cases and Meriq shows what it catches — missing documents, HS / CCC questions, permit risks, and a customer-ready status update.
